skill.name: payload
skill.description: Guides AI assistants through Payload CMS development, including configuration, collections, fields, hooks, access control, APIs, adapters, and plugin patterns.
skill.summary: Build and debug Payload CMS projects with focused TypeScript, access-control, API, and plugin guidance.
skill.author: payloadcms

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but sampled evidence shows Markdown documentation and TypeScript examples, not bundled executable code. No prompt injection, malicious network call, credential exfiltration, or hidden code execution was found. Publish with a warning because the skill contains copyable Payload security examples involving secrets, access control, network calls, email, and filesystem paths.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Empty Secret Fallback
medium_findings.description: A localization example sets the Payload secret from an environment variable with an empty string fallback. If copied into production, this can weaken signing and authentication behavior when the secret is missing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly uses an empty fallback for PAYLOAD_SECRET. The example is documentation, so it is not malicious, but the copied pattern is unsafe.

medium_findings.title: Header-Based IP Access Example Needs Hardening
medium_findings.description: The advanced access-control example reads x-forwarded-for or x-real-ip and compares the raw value to an allowed list. This can fail for CIDR entries and can be spoofed without trusted proxy normalization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly uses proxy headers and a raw includes check. The surrounding note mentions middleware, but the copyable code still lacks normalization and trust-boundary checks.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The large external command count is a false positive. The sampled locations are Markdown tables, fenced bash examples, and TypeScript snippets rather than executable skill scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is rendered documentation. No file reviewed contains a bundled script or shell execution mechanism in the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Examples Are Legitimate but Sensitive
low_findings.description: The skill documents PAYLOAD_SECRET, DATABASE_URL, SMTP_HOST, SMTP_USER, and SMTP_PASS usage. These are normal Payload configuration examples, but generated code must not log, hardcode, or expose those values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are read from environment variables and are not transmitted in the sampled examples. They still require careful handling because they are production secrets.

low_findings.title: External Documentation and Fetch Examples Require User Consent
low_findings.description: The skill links to Payload documentation and includes a fetch call to an example API URL. These are not exfiltration patterns, but assistants should not fetch external resources without user intent.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are Payload resources or example.com, and no credentials are included. The risk is operational network access, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Local API Access-Control Bypass Is Documented as a Pitfall
low_findings.description: The skill shows Payload Local API calls that bypass access control by default and then provides the secure overrideAccess false alternative. This is useful guidance, but users must avoid copying the intentionally wrong example.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The bypass behavior is real and clearly documented as dangerous. Confidence is high because the same warning appears in multiple references with the secure fix.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local API Call Without overrideAccess False
dangerous_patterns.description: Payload Local API operations that pass a user without overrideAccess false can run with elevated privileges. The skill labels this as wrong and provides the secure version.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The unsafe and safe variants are adjacent in the documentation. The pattern is dangerous if copied without the documented correction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Empty PAYLOAD_SECRET Fallback
dangerous_patterns.description: Using an empty string when PAYLOAD_SECRET is absent can create insecure deployments. Production examples should fail closed when required secrets are missing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fallback is visible in the configuration example. It is a content risk rather than evidence of malicious code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Raw Proxy Header IP Check
dangerous_patterns.description: Access rules based directly on x-forwarded-for or x-real-ip need trusted proxy handling, parsing, and CIDR-aware comparison before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example consumes proxy headers directly and compares raw values. That is fragile unless upstream middleware normalizes and validates the client IP.

content.value_statement: Payload CMS projects require precise TypeScript patterns across collections, hooks, access control, and APIs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused references for building and debugging Payload apps.
actual_capabilities: Explains payload.config.ts setup with database adapters, admin config, secrets, and type generation.
Provides collection and field patterns for auth, uploads, drafts, relationships, localization, and custom validation.
Guides Local API, REST, and GraphQL queries with depth, select, where, and relationship examples.
Documents hooks, transactions, req.context, and Next.js revalidation patterns.
Covers access control, RBAC, multi-tenant filtering, custom endpoints, adapters, and plugin development.
limitations: It is reference documentation, not an executable Payload project generator.
Examples may need changes for your Payload version, database, and deployment model.
It cannot verify secrets, database connectivity, or production access rules without project context.
Some examples show unsafe patterns as warnings and should not be copied without the secure alternative.

use_cases.title: Design Secure Collections
use_cases.description: Create Payload collections with fields, relationships, auth settings, and row-level access rules.

use_cases.title: Debug API and Hook Behavior
use_cases.description: Investigate Local API queries, transactions, hook loops, relationship depth, and custom endpoint issues.

use_cases.title: Build Reusable Plugins
use_cases.description: Plan Payload plugin package structure, field injection, hooks, admin components, tests, and distribution patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Collection
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a Payload collection for blog posts with title, slug, author, status, content, and timestamps. Explain each field choice.

prompt_templates.title: Add Access Control
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Payload access-control goal. I need admins to manage all records and authors to manage only their own records.

prompt_templates.title: Debug Hooks and Queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Payload hook and Local API workflow. Identify transaction, access-control, depth, and recursion risks before suggesting fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Plugin Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a Payload plugin that adds configurable fields, preserves user overrides, exports admin components, and supports integration tests.

output_examples.input: I need posts visible only to their author unless the user is an admin.
output: Recommends row-level access control using the authenticated user from the request.
Explains the admin bypass case and when to return a query constraint.
Warns that Local API calls need explicit access enforcement when acting for a user.

output_examples.input: My afterChange hook writes audit records, but failures do not roll back together.
output: Explains that nested Payload operations should receive the same request context.
Checks whether the hook starts separate operations outside the current transaction.
Suggests using context flags to prevent recursive hook behavior.

output_examples.input: I want a plugin that adds fields to selected collections.
output: Outlines a configurable plugin factory with collection matching and override support.
Separates server exports, client admin components, translations, and tests.
Calls out package and build settings that Payload plugins commonly need.

best_practices: Always set overrideAccess false when a Local API call acts on behalf of a user.
Keep PAYLOAD_SECRET, database URLs, and SMTP credentials in managed environment variables.
Pass req through nested Payload operations inside hooks to preserve transactions.
anti_patterns: Copying warning examples that are labeled wrong or insecure.
Using header-based IP checks without trusted proxy validation.
Adding custom endpoints without authentication, input validation, and CORS decisions.

faq.question: What Payload CMS work does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports configuration, collections, fields, access control, hooks, APIs, adapters, endpoints, and plugin development.

faq.question: Can it generate a full Payload application?
faq.answer: It can guide implementation and produce project-specific snippets, but it is primarily a reference skill.

faq.question: Does it cover secure access control?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains role checks, row-level filters, Local API access enforcement, and common bypass mistakes.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. It includes links to Payload documentation, but local reference files cover the main patterns.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What should I review before production use?
faq.answer: Review secrets, access rules, custom endpoints, CORS behavior, hook transactions, and version-specific Payload APIs.
